cards.text: Anwar called Norway's cancellation 'contracts not confetti' and Malaysia is weighing a lawsuit. The first ship those missiles were meant for hasn't been delivered yet.
cards.sub: Norway revoked the Naval Strike Missile export permit in March 2026. LCS-1 KD Maharaja Lela — the lead ship that would have mounted them — only began sea trials in January and isn't scheduled for delivery until December. Malaysia had paid ~RM550m, about 95% of the €124m contract.
sources: Malay Mail, 14 May 2026 — Anwar 'contracts not confetti' statement
USNI News, 14 May 2026 — revocation in March 2026; force majeure
The Star, 14 May 2026 — Khaled Nordin: ~95% paid (~RM500m+)
Kongsberg press release, April 2018 — €124m contract value
Malaysian Defence, 14 May 2026 — LCS-1 sea trials Jan 2026; delivery Dec 2026

cards.h: Zero LCS hulls delivered. RM6.083 billion paid to Boustead Naval Shipyard. Zero convictions across 15 years of investigation.
cards.s: The 2022 PAC report found the programme ceiling ballooned from RM9b to RM11.145b, advance payment to BNS ran at 136× the Treasury threshold, and RM400m of LCS funds was diverted to retire a prior naval programme's debt. The only criminal charge — RM21.08m CBT against ex-BNS MD Tan Sri Ahmad Ramli — ended in a dementia-grounded discharge in March 2025.
cards.d: Parlimen.gov.my PAC media statement 4 Aug 2022; The Star 17 Aug 2022; Malay Mail 5 Feb 2024 & 26 Jul 2024; The Star 13 Mar 2025
sources: Parlimen.gov.my — PAC Chairman Wong Kah Woh media statement 4 Aug 2022 (primary)
Malay Mail 5 Feb 2024 — AGC representation rejection (primary, court filing)
The Star 13 Mar 2025 — Sessions Court DNAA on dementia grounds (primary, judicial)

cards.h: Norway invoked force majeure — an enumerated contract clause, not a breach. The contract worked exactly as it should when a sovereign exporter revokes a licence.
cards.s: Defence-export contracts make export-licence issuance a condition precedent; revocation is not breach by the seller. There is no public precedent of an importer state winning a suit against an exporter state over a revoked arms-export licence — sovereign immunity, the act-of-state doctrine and standard force-majeure clauses combine against such suits.
cards.d: USNI News 14 May 2026; SIPRI commentary on politicisation of export controls 2025; EU Council Common Position 2008/944/CFSP; EJIL:Talk! arms exports access to justice
sources: EU Council Common Position 2008/944/CFSP — EUR-Lex CELEX:32008E0944 (primary)
Wassenaar Arrangement Founding Documents — national-discretion principle (primary)
USNI News 14 May 2026 — MINDEF Sec-Gen Lokman Hakim Ali confirming force majeure invocation
SIPRI 2025 commentary on politicisation of export controls

cards.h: On the same day Malaysia was cut off, Australia signed a Strike Missile Family MoU with Norway for local NSM and JSM production.
cards.s: Malaysia was the only non-NATO and non-NATO-partner customer in Kongsberg's NSM book — every other operator is NATO or partner. Australia, which recognised Palestine in November 2025, received expanded access the same day Malaysia lost it. The discriminator was alliance classification.
cards.d: Army Recognition 14 May 2026 — Australia-Norway Strike Missile Family MoU; Defence Security Asia 14 May 2026; Naval Strike Missile customer list
sources: Army Recognition 14 May 2026 — Strike Missile Family MoU (primary, industry trade)
Defence Security Asia 14 May 2026 — Australia-Malaysia same-day juxtaposition
Kongsberg published customer list — NSM operators by country
Regjeringen.no — Norway Palestine recognition statement, 22 May 2024 (primary)

cards.h: The scandal is not Norway revoking an export licence. The scandal is using Oslo to bury 15 years of unaccounted billions on ships the missiles were never going to fit on.
Like paying 95% upfront for a fitting tailored to a suit that doesn't exist yet — then suing the tailor when his country bans the fabric.
cards.text: LCS-1, the lead ship the missiles were meant for, only began sea trials in January 2026 and is scheduled for delivery in December. The revocation arrived in March, eight months before any ship existed to mount them on.

Norway exercised a clause Malaysia agreed to. The real cost is not the missiles — it is 15 years of LCS payments that produced no ships and no convictions across four governments. Future procurement should price licence-revocation risk into every NATO-platform contract. The unfinished accountability cases on the original LCS spending should not disappear in a foreign-policy news cycle.

sources: Parlimen.gov.my PAC statement 4 Aug 2022 (primary); JKSTUPKK declassified report via Malaysiakini 17 Aug 2022 (primary); AGC filings via Malay Mail Feb–Jul 2024 (primary, judicial); Sessions Court DNAA via The Star Mar 2025 (primary, judicial); Kongsberg press release Apr 2018 (primary, contract); Regjeringen.no Eksportforskriften consolidated text (primary, regulator); Norwegian MFA spokesperson Guri Solberg statement via Reuters/NTB pickup, Breaking Defense, Euronews (primary, government); Regjeringen.no Palestine recognition statement 22 May 2024 (primary, government); EU Council Common Position 2008/944/CFSP (primary, treaty-equivalent); Wassenaar Arrangement Founding Documents (primary); UK Court of Appeal CAAT v Secretary of State [2019] EWCA Civ 1020 (primary, case law); 22 U.S.C. § 2778 (primary, statute); USNI News 14 May 2026; Army Recognition 14 May 2026 (Australia MoU); Naval Today, Naval News, Breaking Defense, Defence Security Asia, Malaysian Defence (specialist defence trade); Malay Mail 14 May 2026; The Star 14 May 2026; NST 14 May 2026; Kosmo Digital 14 May 2026 (BM); Sinar Harian 14 May 2026 (BM); Berita Harian 14 May 2026 (BM); Malaysiakini BM fact-check pieces 14–15 May 2026; C4 Center LCS scandal reporting; The Malaysian Reserve 3 Apr 2024 (Ocean Sunshine takeover); SIPRI 2025 commentary on politicisation of export controls
confidence: High — every load-bearing claim in the cards traces to a primary source in the research brief. The PAC report, JKSTUPKK declassified findings, Ramli court rulings, Kongsberg 2018 contract press release, Norwegian MFA spokesperson statement, and Australia-Norway 14 May 2026 MoU are independently verifiable. The single weak link is whether a US-component (ITAR) re-export restriction was the upstream trigger; this is flagged in needs_verification and not asserted in the cards.
needs_verification: Whether a US-made gyroscope/IMU/GNSS component subject to ITAR re-export restriction was the upstream trigger for Norway's revocation. Sourced via USNI News from an unnamed industry official. Not officially confirmed by Norway or the US. NOT asserted in the cards — but Stage 3 should chase this if any version of it gets introduced.

Khaled Nordin's 'nearly RM1 billion already paid' aggregate figure vs. 'more than RM500 million' / '95% of €124m' on the 2018 contract. The brief uses RM550m on the 2018 contract; the RM1bn aggregate would also include the 2025 USD 11.19m Lekiu launcher contract and likely Kedah-class retrofit pre-payments. Stage 3 should confirm which figure to use if substituted.
The exact date of in-person notification at DSA 2026 (the Palestine/3R research agent reported '20 April 2026'; other accounts say 'mid-April'). The cards do not assert a specific date.

Whether the Norwegian MFA's 'allies and closest partners' formulation has a formally promulgated definition naming country tiers, or remains a policy floor without statutory effect. The research could not locate a formal country-tier list in current Eksportforskriften text.
The precise number of LCS hulls in the current programme (6 originally; LCS-6 quietly dropped to 5 according to Malaysian Defence reporting). The cards do not assert a hull count.
source_notes: Three contradictions were resolved against the brief's authoritative reading. (1) The 'broke a contract' framing in some Malaysian English coverage is rhetorical; the contractual reality is force-majeure invocation, confirmed by MINDEF Sec-Gen Lokman Hakim Ali (USNI News 14 May 2026). The fact card uses force-majeure language. (2) The 'nearly RM1 billion paid' figure aggregates multiple NSM-related contracts; the cleanly attributable 2018 NSM contract is ~RM550m (95% of €124m per Khaled Nordin, 14 May 2026, The Star). The hook uses ~RM550m. (3) The Palestine-causation theory floated by Murray Hunter (The Vibes opinion) and Lam Choong Wah (Malaysiakini analyst quote) is disproved by the Australia same-day MoU and the negative-evidence check on Israeli press; Norwegian Palestine recognition was framed by FM Eide as 'anti-Hamas' — opposite-signed from Malaysia's Hamas engagement. Cards do not assert any Palestine-causation link; Fact 3 actively uses the Australia parallel to disarm that speculation without naming it. Norwegian-language primary press articles (Dagbladet, VG, Aftenposten, Forsvarets Forum, Dagsavisen) were referenced from search-snippets only; direct WebFetch was 403-blocked. A Norwegian-speaking reviewer should cross-check before any Norwegian outlet is quoted verbatim downstream. The 'PA' score below should be read against this constraint — primary-source coverage on the Norwegian regulator side rests on regjeringen.no English-language documents plus Reuters/NTB wire pickup of the MFA spokesperson's English statement, which is adequate but not redundant.
